summary.intent: Fix incorrect tool_result attribution in search results, add content-type filters to the search UI, and improve BM25 relevance scoring to weight messages and thinking more heavily.
summary.outcome: All three goals were completed: tool_result items now always attribute to Claude, a Chip.Group filter bar was added for message/tool_call/tool_result/thinking types, and BM25 weights were updated to 10x for text and thinking columns vs tool-related columns.

repo: Search results include a logEventType field ('user' or 'assistant') and a contentType field ('tool_result', 'thinking', etc.); these two fields together determine proper attribution.
FTS5 index LogContentFts has four columns in order: text (0), thinking (1), toolResultContent (2), toolInput (3), allowing per-column BM25 weighting.
The Search.tsx component performs client-side filtering on already-fetched results, so type filters don't require additional DB queries.

code.finding: isHuman already excluded tool_result via `contentType !== 'tool_result'`, but isClaude did not include tool_result, causing those results to fall through to the user avatar/name in the else branch. Fixed by adding `|| r.contentType === 'tool_result'` to isClaude.
Default FTS5 `rank` was replaced with explicit `bm25(LogContentFts, 10, 10, 1, 1)` to upweight message text and thinking blocks over tool result content and tool inputs.

workflow: FTS5 bm25() function accepts positional per-column weight arguments, enabling fine-grained relevance tuning without schema changes.
Chip.Group is the Mantine pattern used for multi-select filter UIs in this project.

friction: The attribution bug was subtle: isHuman was correctly guarded but isClaude was not, causing tool_result entries to silently fall through to the user branch rather than failing visibly.
open_items: tool_result authorship attribution is currently hardcoded to Claude; if human-run tools are added in the future, the attribution logic will need revisiting to distinguish tool results by who invoked the tool.
BM25 column weights (10, 10, 1, 1) were chosen heuristically; tuning based on real user feedback may be needed.
